title: Notes
abstract: IN reference to the remarks made by M. A. in our last number Mr. T. E. Page writes : ' My object was to deprecate a tendency now prevalent which attaches an exaggerated importance to that part of classical study which consists in the investigation of what may briefly be called "critical" questions. No one in his senses would deny the importance of such investigation : it forms a necessary basis for classical studies.' He urges that the need for his protest is shown by the fact that this year at Cambridge no exercises were sent in for the Greek and Latin ode, and no prize was awarded for Latin hexameters. He attributes this result to that ' blind reverence for German erudition' which has characterised the authorities of Cambridge for the last twenty years. ' You cannot treat scholia and codices, and spelling and pronunciation, and emendation and epigraphy, and archaeology and philology, and all the other things which form the impedimenta of learning as things of primary importance without damaging that living knowledge of the classical writers which is the true object of classical study.' We agree with Mx*. Page to this extent, that the great object of classical education is that the student should be taught to know and to delight in the classical masterpieces : in comparison with this everything else sinks into insignificance. But quis negabit ? Certainly not the erudite Germans. Their system, as is shown in Dr. Ziegler's instructive letter which follows, is carefully framed so as to bring about this very result. The difference between the English and German schoolsystems is not of aim, but of method. In the higher forms of an English school far more time is usually given to the practice of Greek and Latin composition and to the conning of the newest models in each style, more time also to what we may call examination drill; consequently less time is left for the study of the books as wholes. Is it so certain that our method is the better 1 that it succeeds in attaining the desired result in a larger number of cases than the German % Mr. Page recounts the impedimenta with which the University of Cambridge has weighted the study of the classics, and we are disposed to agree that at one time it went too far in insisting on a knowledge of these as an integral part of the examination for the Classical Honours degree. But, as Mr. Page is aware, this has all been changed : archaeology and comparative philology are now optional subjects, which no one need take in unless he has a natural liking for them. One burden there still is from which there is no escape, and which has ere now deterred promising scholars from competing for the Tripos, the burden of compulsory verse composition ; but this, we fear, is a burden which Mr. Page would only bind more closely on the shoulders of all undergraduates.—EDITOR.

title: Editorial Notes
abstract: in Washington, D .C. was very successful. There were over 300 participants-the larges t attendance recorded at a SIGCSE Symposium. The sessions were well attended and the discussions lively-sometimes even spirited. Credit for the success of the meeting must g o to the Symposium Committee. Special thanks go to Jerry Engel, Symposium Chairman, an d to Dick Austing who handled the local arrangements. Plans are already underway for meetings next year. were presented at the Fifth Technical Symposium but were received too late to be included in that issue. The SAM Course Outlines are included in this issue in the hopes of showing some o f the approaches which are being taken at different universities in courses in symbolic an d algebraic manipulation. An earlier contribution on SAM curriculum was published by B. F. I reported in the Bulletin not too long ago (volume 3, number 4, pages 21-23, Decem-ber 1971) the adoption of an oral foreign language requirement for the Ph .D. in compute r science at the University of North Carolina. The only exception was to accept a grade o f B in the fourth semester of French, German, or Russian taken on this campus, where thes e courses emphasize audiolingual skills. It is my duty now to report the subsequent fate o f that requirement. During the first year, several students petitioned successfully to have fourth semester , or later, courses which they had taken accepted in lieu of our courses. Since eac h such student appeared to have a strong case, we found it simpler to amend the requiremen t to permit the substitution of such a course taken elsewhere. As a result, only half a dozen students took the oral language examination in the three years after its adoption a s the " primary" requirement. All passed, one in French and the rest in German. Not only had our oral requirement become de facto primarily a course requirement, bu t many other computer science departments had droppehe language requirement altogether. We became concerned that our weakened requirement was not strong enough to perform a rea l service to our enrolled students, yet strong enough to discourage potential students fro m enrolling (and some applicants had indeed cited it as a reason for going elsewhere). title: Contributions and Comments
abstract: ~ecenf (1Ju6fícâtíons o f ~.~ocr~~~a (POCUbtpi t_qr4p§4.’ WHEN the great Corpus of Apocrypha and Pseudepigrapha appeared a few years ago under the able editorship of Canon Charles, it was almost inaccessible to the scholar who could not afford the I costly and bulky Oxford edition, and it was a / happy thought which prompted Dr. Oesterley and ; Canon .Box to issue a series of handy volumes, clearly and beautifully printed and moderate in price. No less important is the fact that whilst reproducing the older publication the new editors . have reserved for themselves full freedom of action and independence of judgment. They have not been satisfied with merely repeating the results arrived at by former editors, notably those by Canon Charles, to whose industry and learning the study of the apocryphal literature owes so much. This independence of judgment cannot be overestimated. It would have been quite natural for younger scholars to bow before the authority of Canon Charles; this would have led to a school of
